examples.input_text:
The highly anticipated criminal trial for Sam Bankman-Fried, former CEO of bankrupt crypto exchange FTX, started Tuesday to determine whether he’s guilty of seven counts of fraud and conspiracy.
SBF, as he’s known, has pleaded not-guilty to some seven charges of fraud and conspiracy.
The second week of the trial’s standout testimony came from Alameda Research’s former CEO Caroline Ellison, who claimed she took $14 billion from customers to repay debts to lenders, using them as a line of credit under the instruction of SBF.
The prosecution painted Bankman-Fried as someone who knowingly committed fraud to achieve great wealth, power and influence, while the defense countered that the FTX founder acted in good faith, never meant to commit fraud or steal and basically got in over his head.

entities.description: Sam Bankman-Fried, also referred to as SBF and the FTX founder, is the former CEO of the bankrupt crypto exchange FTX. He is the defendant in a highly anticipated criminal trial that started on a Tuesday to determine whether he is guilty of seven counts/charges of fraud and conspiracy. He has pleaded not guilty. The prosecution portrays him as knowingly committing fraud to gain wealth, power, and influence, while the defense argues he acted in good faith, did not intend to commit fraud or steal, and got in over his head.

SBF is the nickname/initialism used in the text for Sam Bankman-Fried, the former CEO and founder of FTX. He is on trial facing seven counts of fraud and conspiracy and has pleaded not guilty to these charges.

Caroline Ellison is the former CEO of Alameda Research. During the second week of Sam Bankman-Fried’s trial, she testified that she took $14 billion from customers to repay debts to lenders, using the customer funds as a line of credit, and stated that she acted under the instruction of SBF.

FTX is a bankrupt crypto exchange whose former CEO and founder is Sam Bankman-Fried. Actions involving FTX customer funds are central to the criminal trial in which Bankman-Fried faces seven counts of fraud and conspiracy.

Alameda Research is a firm whose former CEO is Caroline Ellison. In the context of Sam Bankman-Fried’s trial, Ellison testified that she took $14 billion from customers to repay debts to lenders, treating those customer funds as a line of credit under SBF’s instruction. Alameda Research is closely linked in the testimony to borrowing and repayment activities involving customer funds.

The criminal trial of Sam Bankman-Fried is a highly anticipated legal proceeding that started on a Tuesday. Its purpose is to determine whether he is guilty of seven counts of fraud and conspiracy related to his conduct as founder and former CEO of FTX and its relationship with Alameda Research, including alleged misuse of $14 billion in customer funds. The trial features testimony from figures such as Caroline Ellison and opposing narratives from the prosecution and the defense.

Fraud is one of the main criminal charges brought against Sam Bankman-Fried in the described trial. He faces seven counts of fraud and conspiracy. The prosecution alleges that he knowingly committed fraud to achieve great wealth, power, and influence, including through the alleged misuse of customer funds to repay lender debts, while the defense argues he did not intend to commit fraud or steal.

Conspiracy is another principal criminal charge in the case against Sam Bankman-Fried. He is on trial to determine whether he is guilty of seven counts of fraud and conspiracy, which relate to alleged coordinated wrongful conduct involving customer funds and activities tied to FTX and Alameda Research.

The prosecution in Sam Bankman-Fried’s trial is the side in the criminal case responsible for presenting the case that he committed crimes. In this trial, the prosecution portrays Bankman-Fried as someone who knowingly committed fraud to achieve great wealth, power, and influence.

The defense in Sam Bankman-Fried’s trial is the legal side representing him in the criminal case. The defense narrative is that he acted in good faith, never intended to commit fraud or steal, and essentially got in over his head, in opposition to the prosecution’s portrayal that he knowingly committed fraud.

Fourteen billion dollars is the amount Caroline Ellison testified she took from customers to repay debts to lenders, effectively using customer funds as a line of credit under the instruction of SBF. This sum is central to the allegations about misuse of customer assets discussed at the trial.

The lenders in the Alameda Research context are the entities to whom Alameda Research owed debts. According to Caroline Ellison’s testimony, $14 billion in customer funds were taken and used to repay these lenders, with the customer funds functioning as a line of credit.

entities.entity_name: Second week of Sam Bankman-Fried trial
entities.description: The second week of Sam Bankman-Fried’s trial is the time period during which standout testimony came from Caroline Ellison. During this stage of the proceedings, she stated that she took $14 billion in customer funds to repay lenders under SBF’s instruction.
Tuesday is the day of the week on which Sam Bankman-Fried’s criminal trial started. It marks the commencement day of the proceedings, although no specific calendar date is provided.

entities.entity_name: Seven counts of fraud and conspiracy
entities.description: Seven is the number of counts or charges of fraud and conspiracy that Sam Bankman-Fried faces in the trial. The text notes that the trial aims to determine whether he is guilty of seven counts of fraud and conspiracy and that he has pleaded not guilty to these charges.

examples.input_text:
“Certainly there wasn’t a valid alternative to Google at the time,” Cue said.
Sullivan is mad that the public and the media don’t really understand what he considers to be basic precepts about how search works, leading him to adopt a rather scolding tone online.

entities.description: A technology company described as having no valid alternative in the context of internet search at the time being discussed, and referenced as the dominant search provider compared to other options.

An individual identified only by the surname Cue, quoted as saying that there was not a valid alternative to Google at the time, providing an opinion or testimony about the search market and Google’s position in it.

An individual identified only by the surname Sullivan, described as being angry that the public and the media do not understand what he considers basic precepts about how search works, which leads him to adopt a scolding tone online.
The general concept of how internet search works; Sullivan believes there are basic precepts about how search functions that the public and the media fail to understand.

examples.input_text:
In blog posts and press materials, Google touted Gemini’s superior architecture and capabilities, claiming that the model meets or exceeds the performance of other leading gen AI models like OpenAI’s GPT-4.
In our last roundup, we learned how Google spent $26.3 billion in 2021 making itself the default search engine across platforms and how Google tried to have Chrome preinstalled on iPhones.
When Google’s search head Prabhakar Raghavan testified in court on October 28, he revealed that the tech giant had paid $26.3 billion in 2021 to multiple browsers, phones and platforms, from companies including Apple, Samsung and Mozilla, The Verge reports.
The case, filed by Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle, argues that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, their readers and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.

entities.description: Google is a large technology company that developed the Gemini AI model, claimed in blog posts and press materials that Gemini’s architecture and capabilities meet or exceed other leading generative AI models, spent and paid a total of $26.3 billion in 2021 to make itself the default search engine across platforms (including payments to multiple browsers, phones and platforms from companies such as Apple, Samsung and Mozilla), attempted to have its Chrome browser preinstalled on iPhones, and is accused in a legal case of siphoning off news publishers’ content, readers and ad revenue through allegedly anticompetitive practices.

Gemini is a generative AI model created by Google. In blog posts and press materials, Google touted Gemini’s superior architecture and capabilities and claimed that the model meets or exceeds the performance of other leading generative AI models like OpenAI’s GPT-4.

OpenAI is an artificial intelligence organization referenced as the creator of GPT-4. Google compares its Gemini model to OpenAI’s GPT-4, claiming Gemini meets or exceeds the performance of leading generative AI models such as GPT-4.

GPT-4 is a generative AI model created by OpenAI. It is mentioned as one of the leading generative AI models, and Google claims that its Gemini model meets or exceeds the performance of models like GPT-4.

The year 2021 is referenced as the time period in which Google spent and paid a total of $26.3 billion to make itself the default search engine across platforms, including payments to multiple browsers, phones and platforms from companies such as Apple, Samsung and Mozilla.

An amount of $26.3 billion that Google spent or paid in 2021. It is described as the cost Google incurred to make itself the default search engine across platforms and as payments made to multiple browsers, phones and platforms, including those from Apple, Samsung and Mozilla.
Chrome is Google’s web browser. The text states that Google tried to have Chrome preinstalled on iPhones as part of its efforts to secure distribution and default search status.

iPhones are smartphones designed and sold by Apple. The text notes that Google tried to have its Chrome browser preinstalled on iPhones as part of arrangements related to search and browser distribution.

Prabhakar Raghavan is the head of search at Google. He testified in court on October 28 and revealed that Google had paid $26.3 billion in 2021 to multiple browsers, phones and platforms from companies including Apple, Samsung and Mozilla.
October 28 is the date on which Google’s search head, Prabhakar Raghavan, testified in court and disclosed that Google paid $26.3 billion in 2021 to multiple browsers, phones and platforms.

Apple is a technology company mentioned as one of the companies whose browsers, phones or platforms received part of the $26.3 billion that Google paid in 2021 to secure default search engine status and related arrangements.

Samsung is a technology company cited as one of the companies whose browsers, phones or platforms were paid as part of the $26.3 billion Google spent in 2021 to be the default search engine and for related placements.

Mozilla is an organization mentioned as one of the companies whose browsers, phones or platforms received part of the $26.3 billion Google paid in 2021 to secure default search engine or similar placement.

The Verge is a media outlet that reported on Prabhakar Raghavan’s court testimony regarding Google’s $26.3 billion payments in 2021 to browsers, phones and platforms from companies including Apple, Samsung and Mozilla.

entities.entity_name: Legal case filed by Helena World Chronicle against Google
entities.description: A legal case filed by Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle against Google. The case alleges that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, their readers and advertising revenue through anticompetitive means. The name of the case is not explicitly given in the text, but it concerns alleged anticompetitive behavior related to news content and advertising.

Helena World Chronicle is an Arkansas-based publisher that filed a legal case against Google. The publisher argues that Google siphons off news publishers’ content, their readers and advertising revenue through allegedly anticompetitive practices.
Arkansas is a U.S. state identified as the location where the publisher Helena World Chronicle is based.

The phrase “anticompetitive means” refers to the allegedly unfair, anti-competitive practices that Helena World Chronicle claims Google uses to siphon news publishers’ content, readers and advertising revenue. It characterizes the nature of the alleged conduct in the legal case.

News publishers are organizations that produce news content. In the text, Helena World Chronicle claims that Google siphons off the content, readers and ad revenue that belong to news publishers through allegedly anticompetitive practices; they are described as a group that is harmed by the alleged conduct.

examples.input_text:

The case, filed by Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle, argues that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, their readers and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.

An amount of 26.3 billion US dollars that Google spent in 2021 to make itself the default search engine across platforms, as revealed during the Monday trial hearing.

A multinational technology company that, according to the text, spent $26.3 billion in 2021 to make its search engine the default across platforms, tried to have its Chrome browser preinstalled on iPhones, and is accused in a legal case of siphoning off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.

The status of being the automatically selected search service on various platforms; in the text, it refers to Google making itself the default search engine across platforms by spending $26.3 billion in 2021.
The year in which Google spent $26.3 billion to make itself the default search engine across platforms, as mentioned in the trial hearing.
Google’s web browser, referred to simply as Chrome in the text, which Google tried to have preinstalled on iPhones as part of extending its reach beyond default search engine status.
Apple’s line of smartphones on which Google attempted to have its Chrome browser preinstalled, in addition to making its search engine the default across platforms.

entities.entity_name: Case filed by Helena World Chronicle against Google
entities.description: A legal case brought by the Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle against Google, arguing that Google siphons off news publishers’ content, their readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.
An Arkansas-based publisher that filed a legal case against Google, claiming that Google siphons off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.
A U.S. state mentioned as the location where the publisher Helena World Chronicle is based.

Organizations that produce news content; according to the case described in the text, Google is alleged to siphon off these publishers’ content, readers, and advertising revenue through anticompetitive means.
Readers or audience members of news publishers whose attention is alleged, in the case against Google, to be siphoned off by Google along with content and ad revenue.

Money earned from advertisements; in the case filed by Helena World Chronicle, this advertising revenue is alleged to be siphoned away from news publishers by Google through anticompetitive practices.

Unfair business practices that restrict competition; the case by Helena World Chronicle alleges that Google uses such anticompetitive means to siphon off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue.

While Israel has granted permits to about 17,000 Gaza residents to enter and work in Israel, the food, fuel and medical supplies that people in Gaza use all first pass through Israel.

entities.description: An unspecified user referenced in the text who states that no one can carry out a major attack against Israel without intelligence support. No further identifying details are provided.

A country in the Middle East that is described in the text as the potential target of a major attack, as the state that grants work permits to Gaza residents to enter and work there, and as the authority through which food, fuel, and medical supplies used by people in Gaza must first pass.

A territory whose residents receive work permits from Israel to enter and work in Israel. The text states that the food, fuel, and medical supplies that people in Gaza use all first pass through Israel.
A significant hostile operation against Israel. The text mentions the opinion that no one can carry out such a major attack against Israel without intelligence support.
Permissions granted by Israel that allow residents of Gaza to enter and work in Israel. The text notes that Israel has granted about 17,000 such permits.
An approximate quantity given in the text for the number of Gaza residents who have been granted permits to enter and work in Israel.
Edible goods mentioned as one of the types of supplies used by people in Gaza that must first pass through Israel before reaching Gaza.
Combustible material used as an energy source, mentioned as one of the types of supplies used by people in Gaza that must first pass through Israel before reaching Gaza.

examples.input_text:
She admits that she may have misinterpreted the evidence, but maintains that Google manipulates Search to maximise ad revenue.
This whole line of argument is called “market definition.” If the court decides the relevant market is phones and app stores, not specifically Android ones, then Google’s on the road to victory.
Google unveils new hardware: This week was Google’s annual hardware event, where the search and consumer tech giant showed off what it’s been working on.
The case, filed by Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle, argues that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, their readers and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.

entities.description: Google is described as a search and consumer technology giant that operates the Google Search engine and advertising business. In the text, it is accused of manipulating Search to maximise ad revenue, is said to be potentially “on the road to victory” depending on how the court defines the relevant market, and is alleged to siphon off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means. Google also holds an annual hardware event where it unveils new hardware products and shows what it has been working on.
Google Search is the search engine product operated by Google. In the text, Google is said to manipulate Search in order to maximise advertising revenue.

Market definition is an economic and legal concept and line of argument used in competition and antitrust cases to define what the relevant market is. In the text, it refers to the argument over whether the relevant market should be defined broadly as phones and app stores or more narrowly as Android ones, a choice that affects Google’s chances of winning the case.

Phones are mobile phone devices that are part of a potential relevant market in the legal argument described. The text notes that if the court defines the relevant market broadly as phones and app stores, rather than specifically Android ones, Google is more likely to be on the road to victory.

App stores are digital distribution platforms for applications on mobile devices. In the text, they form part of the broader market category (“phones and app stores”) that the court may consider when determining the relevant market in the case involving Google.

Android is a mobile operating system. In the text, “Android ones” refers to Android phones and/or Android app stores as a narrower potential relevant market; if the court defines the relevant market specifically as Android ones rather than all phones and app stores, it would change the competitive assessment involving Google.

Google’s annual hardware event is a recurring event hosted by Google where it unveils new hardware products and shows what it has been working on. In the text, it took place during the referenced week and is mentioned as the venue where Google unveiled new hardware.

Helena World Chronicle is an Arkansas-based news publisher. In the text, it is identified as the publisher that filed a legal case against Google, arguing that Google siphons off news publishers’ content, their readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.
Arkansas is a state in the United States. In the text, it is identified as the state where the publisher Helena World Chronicle is based.

News publishers are organizations that produce and distribute news content. In the text, they are described as victims of Google’s alleged practices, with Helena World Chronicle alleging that Google siphons off news publishers’ content, their readers, and advertising revenue through anticompetitive means.

Ad revenue refers to advertising income earned from displaying advertisements. In the text, Google is accused of manipulating Search to maximise ad revenue and of siphoning off news publishers’ ad revenue.

Anticompetitive means refers to methods or practices that unfairly limit competition in a market. In the text, Helena World Chronicle alleges that Google uses anticompetitive means to siphon off news publishers’ content, their readers, and ad revenue.

For all the other benefits they get from Android, Google could solve this problem today if they wanted to.
This whole line of argument is called “market definition.” If the court decides the relevant market is phones and app stores, not specifically Android ones, then Google’s on the road to victory.
The case, filed by Arkansas-based publisher Helena World Chronicle, argues that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, their readers and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.

entities.description: A large technology company that, according to the text, spent $26.3 billion in 2021 to make itself the default search engine across platforms, tried to have its Chrome browser preinstalled on iPhones, can use benefits from Android to address an identified problem, is involved in a legal case where the court’s market-definition decision (phones and app stores vs. specifically Android ones) will affect its prospects, and is accused of siphoning off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue through anticompetitive means.
The amount of money Google spent in 2021 to make itself the default search engine across platforms, as revealed at a Monday trial hearing.

A web browser developed by Google; the text states that Google tried to have Chrome preinstalled on iPhones as part of efforts to extend its search dominance.

Android is also central to a distinction in the legal case’s market definition (Android phones and app stores vs. a broader market).

The legal and economic concept described in the text as the line of argument about how to define the relevant competitive market. The court must decide whether the relevant market is phones and app stores generally or specifically Android ones, which will influence Google’s chances in the case.

Software platforms for distributing and selling mobile applications, cited as part of the possible relevant market (“phones and app stores”) in the court’s analysis of the case involving Google.
A U.S. state identified in the text as the location of the publisher Helena World Chronicle, which filed the case against Google.
A publisher based in Arkansas that filed the case against Google, alleging that Google “siphons off” news publishers’ content, readers, and advertising revenue through anticompetitive means.
News organizations that produce news content; the case claims that Google siphons off these publishers’ content, their readers, and advertising revenue through anticompetitive means.

The methods characterized in the lawsuit as anticompetitive, alleged to be the means by which Google siphons off news publishers’ content, readers, and ad revenue; the text does not specify particular statutes or offenses beyond describing the conduct as anticompetitive.
